Looking at Chile more carefully, the red area is very prominent on the left side (1965) and becomes almost invisible by around 1990s-2000s. This is a very dramatic drop over about 20-30 years.

content.text:
<think>
Looking at the complete chart image, I can see two rows of country alcohol consumption data from 1965-2015. The red section represents spirits consumption.

Examining each country:
- **Chile** (bottom row, first position): The red (spirits) area starts quite large/substantial on the left side (around 1965) and drops dramatically to almost invisible by the right side (around 2015). This is a very steep decline.
- **Brazil** (top row, fourth position): Also shows some reduction in the red area, but not as dramatic as Chile.
- All other countries (Australia, Austria, Belgium, Canada, China, Costa Rica, Cuba, Czechia): Show relatively stable or minimal spirits consumption throughout the period.

Chile's spirits consumption shows the most dramatic visual drop - going from a significant portion of the consumption stack to nearly zero over the ~50 year period.
</think>

<answer>
**Chile** shows the most dramatic drop in the percentage of spirits consumption. The red (spirits) area is substantial in 1965 but drops dramatically to almost nothing by 2015, representing a severe decline in spirits consumption relative to other beverages over this period.
</answer>
